./sessions/30ed5d58-e06e-47c0-9b93-a54b68002c06.stdout:8:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_3","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,240p' .pod-codex-home/skills/agent-worker-flow/SKILL.md\"","aggregated_output":"---\nname: agent-worker-flow\ndescription: Standard claim/branch/verify/publish workflow for pod agent sessions. Read this skill at the start of any feature, review, summarize, or meditate session.\nallowed-tools: Bash, Read, Glob, Grep\n---\n\n# Standard Worker Flow for Pod Agent Sessions\n\nThis skill covers the shared workflow used by all pod worker agents.\nSession-specific commands reference this skill rather than duplicating it.\n\n## Coordination Reference\n\nThe `coordination` script handles all GitHub-based multi-agent coordination.\nSession UUID is available as `$POD_SESSION_ID` (exported by `pod`).\nThe `gh` CLI defaults to the current repo, so `--repo` is not needed.\n\n| Command | What it does |\n|---------|-------------|\n| `coordination orient` | List unclaimed/claimed issues, open PRs, PRs needing attention |\n| `coordination plan [--label L] \"title\"` | Create GitHub issue with agent-plan + optional label; body from stdin |\n| `coordination create-pr N [--partial] [\"title\"]` | Push branch, create PR closing issue #N, enable auto-merge, swap `claimed` → `has-pr`. With `--partial`: adds `replan` label. |\n| `coordination claim-fix N` | Comment on failing PR #N claiming fix (30min cooldown) |\n| `coordination close-pr N \"reason\"` | Comment reason and close PR #N |\n| `coordination list-unclaimed [--label L]` | List unclaimed agent-plan issues (FIFO order); optional label filter |\n| `coordination queue-depth [L]` | Count of unclaimed issues; optional label for per-type count |\n| `coordination claim N` | Claim issue #N — adds `claimed` label + comment, detects races |\n| `coordination skip N \"reason\"` | Mark claimed issue as needing replan — removes `claimed`, adds `replan` label |\n| `coordination add-dep N M` | Add `depends-on: #M` to issue #N's body; adds `blocked` label if #M is open |\n| `coordination check-blocked` | Unblock issues whose `depends-on` dependencies are all closed |\n| `coordination release-stale-claims [SECS]` | Release claimed issues with no PR after SECS seconds (default 4h); **manual use only** |\n| `coordination lock-planner` | Acquire advisory planner lock (20min TTL) |\n| `coordination unlock-planner` | Release planner lock early |\n| `coordination critical-path-depth [L]` | Count unclaimed critical-path issues; optional label filter |\n| `coordination set-target N` | Planner sets recommended target agent count (wind-down use only) |\n\n**Issue lifecycle**: planner creates issue (label: `agent-plan`) →\nworker claims it (adds label: `claimed`) → worker creates PR closing it\n(label swaps to `has-pr`) → auto-merge squash-merges.\nIssues marked `replan` (by skip, partial completion, or worker-led\ndecomposition) are handled by the next planner. Issues with `has-pr` are\nexcluded from `list-unclaimed` and `queue-depth`.\n\n**Partial completion**: worker uses `--partial` → label swaps to\n`replan`. A planner creates a new issue for remaining work, then closes\nthe `replan` issue with a link to the new one.\n\n**Worker-led decomposition**: if the claimed issue is too large for one\nsession, worker creates sub-issues and `coordination skip`s the parent\nwith a `Decomposed into #X, #Y` breadcrumb comment. The next planner\neither closes the parent (sub-issues fully cover it) or narrows it to the\nresidual scope. See \"Assess Scope\" (Step 4b) for the full procedure.\n\n**Dependencies**: Issues can declare `depends-on: #N` in their body.\n`coordination plan` auto-adds the `blocked` label if any dependency is\nopen. `check-blocked` (run by `pod` each loop) removes `blocked` when\nall dependencies close. Blocked issues are excluded from\n`list-unclaimed` and `queue-depth`.\n\n**Branch naming**: `agent/<first-8-chars-of-UUID>`\n**Plan files**: `plans/<UUID-prefix>.md`\n**Progress files**: `progress/<UTC-timestamp>_<UUID-prefix>.md`\n\n## Step 1: Claim a Work Item\n\n```\ncoordination orient\n```\n\n**Priority order:**\n0. **Human oversight directives first**: Check for open `human-oversight` issues before\n   anything else. These are direct instructions from the project owner and take absolute\n   precedence over all other work:\n   ```\n   coordination list-unclaimed --label human-oversight\n   ```\n   If any are open and unclaimed, claim the oldest one immediately.\n   **These issues cannot be skipped or refused because you disagree with the approach.**\n   The only valid exit from a `human-oversight` issue is completing it, or posting a\n   comment explaining a genuine technical blocker (e.g. a missing dependency), then\n   using `coordination skip` with that reason. Do not `skip` because you think a\n   different approach is better — that is the owner's call, not yours.\n1. **Oldest unclaimed issue** of your type:\n   ```\n   coordination list-unclaimed --label <your-label>\n   ```\n\n**Don't repair PRs from a worker session.** PR health (merge conflicts,\nfailed CI, stuck CI) is the `repair` agent's responsibility; pod dispatches\n`/repair` automatically when `coordination list-pr-repair` reports\ncandidates, ahead of `/plan` / `/work`. Focus on fresh issue work.\n\nIf the queue is empty, write a brief progress note and exit.\n\n```\ncoordination claim <issue-number>\n```\n\n**You MUST check the output.** If it says `CLAIM FAILED`, you MUST NOT work\non that issue — pick a different one. Only proceed if the output says\n`Claimed issue #N`. Read the full issue body:\n```\ngh issue view <N> --json body --jq .body\n```\n\n## Step 2: Set Up\n\n```bash\ngit checkout -b agent/<first-8-chars-of-session-UUID>\ngit rev-parse HEAD      # record starting commit\n```\n\n**If the branch already exists** (common in reused worktrees): check for an\nopen PR on it first (`gh pr list --head agent/<id>`). If a PR exists, create\na new branch with a suffix (`agent/<id>-v2`). If no PR exists, reset it to\nmaster: `git checkout agent/<id> && git reset --hard origin/master`.\n\nRecord any project-specific quality metrics (e.g. sorry count, test coverage)\nas described in the project's CLAUDE.md.\n\n## Step 3: Codebase Orientation\n\nRead the specific files mentioned in the plan/issue. Understand the current state\nof code you'll be modifying. Don't read progress history — the issue body provides\nthat context.\n\n## Step 4: Verify Assumptions\n\nCheck that the plan's assumptions still hold:\n- Quality metrics match what the issue says\n- Files mentioned in the issue still exist and haven't been restructured\n- No recently merged PR invalidates the plan\n\nIf stale:\n```\ncoordination skip <issue-number> \"reason: <what changed>\"\n```\nGo back to Step 1 and try the next issue.\n\n**PR fix plans**: If the plan asks you to fix a broken PR, use judgement. If the\nPR is low quality or not worth salvaging:\n```\ncoordination close-pr <pr-number> \"reason: <why not worth fixing>\"\n```\n\n## Step 4b: Assess Scope\n\nAfter orienting but **before writing code**, check whether the task fits\nin a single session. Warning signs it doesn't:\n\n- Target file is 500+ lines and you need to understand most of it\n- The work naturally splits into independent sub-lemmas or sub-tasks\n- Difficulty feels higher than the issue says\n\nIf the issue is too large, **decomposing it into smaller sub-issues is a\nnormal success path**, not a failure mode. You have the freshest codebase\ncontext and can usually scope sub-tasks more accurately than a planner could\nin advance. A good decomposition is more valuable than a failed heroic\nattempt — and far better than overrunning the session trying to salvage it.\n\nYou may decompose when any of these is true:\n- the claimed issue is too large for one session,\n- the work naturally splits into independent sub-tasks,\n- you can write self-contained successor issues without further investigation.\n\n```bash\n# 1. Create self-contained sub-issues. Use `coordination plan` exactly as a\n#    planner would — same body template (Current state / Deliverables /\n#    Context / Verification), same label. Note: `coordination plan` does\n#    only best-effort title-keyword overlap warnings; it does not hold the\n#    planner lock and cannot atomically dedupe against concurrent creators.\n#    If you see open issues that look related, link or coordinate\n#    explicitly in the sub-issue body rather than relying on the warning.\necho \"body...\" | coordination plan --label feature \"Sub-task 1: ...\"\necho \"body...\" | coordination plan --label feature \"Sub-task 2: ...\"\n\n# 2. Link ordering dependencies if any sub-task must precede another.\n#    Do NOT add `depends-on: #<parent>` — the parent is about to be\n#    superseded; depend on real predecessor sub-issues instead.\ncoordination add-dep <sub2> <sub1>\n\n# 3. Leave a machine-readable breadcrumb on the parent. The planner's\n#    replan-triage step keys off this exact `Decomposed into #X, #Y`\n#    phrasing — keep it on a single line at the start of the comment.\ngh issue comment <parent> --body \"Decomposed into #<sub1>, #<sub2>\n\n(reason: <one-line scope assessment>)\"\n\n# 4. Release the claim by marking the parent for planner triage. This\n#    routes through pod's claim-state machinery and clears the in-process\n#    `state.claimed_issue` correctly. Do NOT use `gh issue close` directly:\n#    it leaves pod's session-end cleanup thinking the issue is still\n#    claimed and will attempt a stray `coordination skip` on a closed\n#    issue at exit.\ncoordination skip <parent> \"Decomposed into #<sub1>, #<sub2> — see comment\"\n```\n\nThe planner's next replan-triage cycle picks the parent up and either\ncloses it (if the sub-issues fully cover it) or narrows the body to the\nresidual scope (if not).\n\nAfter decomposing, you have two options:\n\n1. **Continue on one of the sub-issues**: claim it via `coordination claim`,\n   then return to Step 2 with the sub-issue. Common case when the parent\n   was just two work items glued together.\n2. **Stop and exit**: if you've used most of your session orienting, write a\n   brief progress entry and exit. The next worker will claim a sub-issue.\n\nIf you've already done a coherent subset of the parent's work *before*\ndeciding to decompose, prefer the partial-PR path:\n\n```bash\n# Steps 1-3 above (create sub-issues for the remaining work, leave the\n# `Decomposed into #X, #Y` breadcrumb on the parent).\n\n# Then land your coherent subset. `--partial` marks the parent `replan`.\ncoordination create-pr <parent> --partial \"feat: <what landed>\"\n```\n\nThe next planner sees the breadcrumb on the parent and closes it with a\nforward link to the sub-issues.\n\n## Step 5: Execute\n\nAfter each coherent chunk of changes:\n- Build and test using the project's build commands (see project CLAUDE.md)\n- Commit with conventional prefixes: `feat:`, `fix:`, `refactor:`, `test:`, `doc:`, `chore:`\n\nEach commit must compile. One logical change per commit.\n\n**Commit early, create PRs early.** Sessions can terminate at any time.\nPushed-but-not-PR'd work is effectively lost — nobody will find it.\n\n- Commit after every compiling milestone. Don't wait for the full feature.\n- WIP commits are fine: `feat: WIP prove helper_lemma (2/4 sorries remain)`\n- If 20+ minutes have passed without a commit, stop and commit now.\n- Use `coordination create-pr N --partial` as soon as you have useful\n  progress, even if incomplete. This saves the work as a visible PR.\n\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/30ed5d58-e06e-47c0-9b93-a54b68002c06.stdout:12:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_5","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' PLAN.md\"","aggregated_output":"# hex — Workflow Plan\n\nYou are a planner agent. Your job is to **survey project state and\ndispatch GitHub issues** for worker agents to pick up. You do not\nimplement — workers do.\n\n## Start of cycle\n\n1. Merge all mergeable + green open PRs before creating new work.\n   Details in [PLAN/Conventions.md](PLAN/Conventions.md).\n2. List current open GitHub issues. These are your ground truth for\n   work already in flight; you will cross-reference against the\n   status survey below to avoid duplicate dispatch.\n\n## Bootstrap check\n\nIf `scripts/status.py` does not yet exist, the repo is pre-bootstrap.\nGo straight to [PLAN/Phase0.md](PLAN/Phase0.md) — Phase 0 is a single\ncritical-path issue handled by one worker. Dispatch (or execute) it\nand wait for it to land before returning here.\n\n## Survey and dispatch\n\nOtherwise, run `scripts/status.py`. It lists every `(library, phase)`\npair that is currently ready for work, and for each pair names:\n\n- the **SPEC file**: `SPEC/Libraries/hex-foo.md` — the library's design;\n- the **PLAN file**: `PLAN/PhaseK.md` — the phase rules and exit criteria;\n- the `libraries.yml` bump expected when the phase completes.\n\nMultiple pairs are ready at once — that is the point of the\nphase-dependency rules. Dispatch in parallel rather than serialising.\nFor each ready pair, decide whether to create new issues using:\n\n- **Existing open-issue coverage.** If open issues already cover a\n  pair adequately, skip it this cycle.\n- **Worker capacity headroom.** Don't dispatch more than workers can\n  reasonably pick up before the next planner cycle.\n\nFor each pair you do choose to dispatch:\n\n1. Read `SPEC/Libraries/hex-foo.md` for the library's design (and\n   `SPEC/SPEC.md` + any cross-cutting SPEC files it links — e.g.\n   `SPEC/testing.md` for Phase 3 — if you haven't already this session).\n2. Read `PLAN/PhaseK.md` for the phase's scope and exit criteria.\n3. **Add more narrow GitHub issues** advancing the pair's work. You\n   are not writing a complete decomposition for the `(library,\n   phase)` pair in one go — you are adding the next batch of work\n   units for workers to pick up, on top of whatever issues already\n   exist. Follow the issue-creation rules in\n   [PLAN/Conventions.md](PLAN/Conventions.md) (canonical body\n   shape, narrow scope, `depends-on:` dependency syntax).\n\n## Once per session\n\nRead [PLAN/Conventions.md](PLAN/Conventions.md) for conventions you\nshouldn't rediscover each time: naming, FFI, PR auto-merge, per-turn\nprogress files, `SPEC/` immutability, the phase-dependency rule\ntable, and how `libraries.yml` relates to `status/` tokens and\nGitHub issues.\n\n## Key files\n\n- [SPEC/SPEC.md](SPEC/SPEC.md) and [SPEC/Libraries/](SPEC/Libraries/)\n  — *what* to build.\n- [libraries.yml](libraries.yml) — per-library phase counter.\n- [PLAN/](PLAN/) — *how* to execute each phase; immutable reference.\n- [PLAN/Releases.md](PLAN/Releases.md) — release readiness predicate.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/30ed5d58-e06e-47c0-9b93-a54b68002c06.stdout:14:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_6","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' SPEC/SPEC.md\"","aggregated_output":"# hex — Verified Computational Algebra in Lean 4\n\nA collection of cooperating Lean 4 libraries providing performant, verified\nalgorithms for computational algebra: polynomial arithmetic, factoring,\nirreducibility testing, finite field construction, lattice basis reduction,\nand related tools.\n\n## What we're building\n\nThe end state is a verified Berlekamp-Zassenhaus factoring pipeline for\npolynomials over the integers, with LLL lattice basis reduction for the\nfactor recombination step. All algorithms are implemented and run natively\nin Lean 4 — no external CAS in the loop. The pipeline produces machine-checked\nproofs of correctness alongside its computational results.\n\nThe computational core is Mathlib-free: dense `Array`-backed polynomials with\n`UInt64` coefficients for finite-field arithmetic, Barrett/Montgomery reduction\nfor modular operations, and GMP FFI for big-integer primitives. Separate\nMathlib bridge libraries prove correspondence with Mathlib's mathematical\ndefinitions (e.g. `DensePoly R ≃+* Polynomial R`, `ZMod64 p ≃+* ZMod p`,\n`GFq p n ≃+* GaloisField p n`), transferring deep correctness results from\nMathlib's abstract algebra without imposing Mathlib as a dependency on the\ncomputational code.\n\nThe library DAG has three independent roots — polynomial arithmetic, integer\narithmetic, and matrix operations — meeting at the top in\nBerlekamp-Zassenhaus. This structure allows parallel development: LLL has no\ndependency on polynomial arithmetic, Hensel lifting is independent of LLL,\nand all proof work is fully parallelizable once theorem statements are in\nplace.\n\n## Project-wide proof policy\n\n`native_decide` is banned throughout the project. Large computational\nproofs must be carried by explicit verified checkers or tactics with\nstable, benchmarked runtime characteristics, rather than by delegating\nproof checking to `native_decide`.\n\n`@[extern]` is allowed only for runtime hooks explicitly called for in\nthe SPEC. New trusted extern boundaries must not be invented during\nimplementation. For each approved extern, the corresponding library spec\nshould state the intended contract, any fallback path, and any relevant\nplatform assumptions.\n\n**Property all `@[extern]` declarations must satisfy.** The C body of\nevery `@[extern]` declaration must do work that is not equivalent to\ncalling its Lean fallback. An `@[extern]` shim whose only effect is to\nre-enter the Lean runtime — for example a `lean_hex_foo` whose body is\n`return l_Hex_pureFoo___boxed(a, b);` — is not a valid extern boundary.\nUntil the C side performs work native to C (GMP call, CLMUL intrinsic,\n`__uint128_t` arithmetic, etc.), ship only the pure-Lean definition\nwithout `@[extern]`.\n\n## Applications\n\n**Cryptographic field construction:** To build `GF(2^128)` for AES, you\nneed an irreducible polynomial of degree 128 over `F_2`. With\nhex-berlekamp, produce a Lean proof that it's irreducible.\n\n**Coding theory:** Reed-Solomon and BCH codes need irreducible polynomials\nover finite fields. Verified factoring provides certified generator\npolynomials.\n\n**Number theory:** Computing rings of integers requires factoring\npolynomials over Z. The Baanen et al. project currently delegates to\nSageMath with unverified certificates — this replaces that dependency.\n\n**Cryptanalysis:** LLL is the core tool for lattice-based attacks. A\nverified LLL gives confidence in attack results.\n\n## Navigation\n\n- [Design principles](design-principles.md)\n- [Lean 4 stdlib inventory](lean4-stdlib-inventory.md)\n- [Libraries](Libraries/) (DAG + per-library docs)\n- [Tutorials](tutorials.md)\n- [Testing](testing.md)\n- [Benchmarking](benchmarking.md)\n- [Prior art](prior-art.md)\n- [Future work](future-work.md)\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/30ed5d58-e06e-47c0-9b93-a54b68002c06.stdout:47:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_25","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' PLAN/Phase2.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Phase 2: Scaffolding Review — \"What Are We Missing?\"\n\n**Coupling:** local. Library L can start Phase 2 once\n`libraries.yml[L].done_through ≥ 1`. Cross-library state is irrelevant.\n\nAfter a library is scaffolded, create issues for **skeptical reviews**.\nThese are not rubber-stamp coverage audits — they ask:\n\n> *What essential functionality is missing that downstream libraries\n> will need?*\n\n## Review questions\n\n- Does hex-arith expose everything hex-mod-arith needs?\n- Does hex-poly's `DensePoly` API support the operations hex-poly-fp\n  requires?\n- Are there lemmas about intermediate quantities that the SPEC doesn't\n  mention but the proof strategies implicitly require?\n- Are the theorem statements faithful to the SPEC? (Not \"verbatim\" — but\n  do they capture the same mathematical content?)\n- Are there missing imports or DAG violations?\n- **Does every committed `def` / data-carrying `structure` field /\n  `class` / `instance` body actually implement the SPEC contract\n  correctly?** [PLAN/Phase1.md](Phase1.md) forbids data-level\n  placeholders of any kind — no `sorry` bodies, no `axiom`s\n  standing in for data, no trivial returns (input unchanged, `0`,\n  `Matrix.identity`, `none`, empty list). A library must commit\n  only the declarations it can correctly implement; everything\n  else stays out of Lean until a later PR implements it. Flag any\n  committed declaration whose body is a placeholder in any of\n  these forms. Flagged bodies must be deleted (and any downstream\n  callers updated) in a follow-up issue before the\n  `scaffolding-reviewed` token is committed — either the\n  implementation becomes correct, or the declaration leaves the\n  tree. \"Rewrite as `sorry`\" is **not** an acceptable fix.\n- **Does each data-level body match the SPEC's intended algorithmic\n  shape — not just its input/output contract?** Flag any body that\n  produces the right output via an unrelated algorithm: a full\n  rebuild where the SPEC prescribes an in-place update; a\n  reference implementation behind a name that promises the\n  optimised version; a detour through canonical form when the SPEC\n  gives explicit update formulas. These are \"wrong-shape\" scaffolds\n  and are forbidden by [PLAN/Phase1.md](Phase1.md), same remedy as\n  wrong-output scaffolds: fix the body, or delete the declaration.\n- **For each `@[extern]` in the library, open its C body in\n  `HexFoo/ffi/<name>.c` and confirm it does work native to C.** A C\n  body that consists of (or reduces to) `return l_Hex_*___boxed(a,\n  b);` — i.e. a trampoline back to the Lean runtime — is a fake\n  extern. Remedy: delete the `@[extern]` attribute, delete the C\n  file, drop the `moreLinkArgs`/`moreLeancArgs` entry in the\n  lakefile, and open a follow-up issue for the real extern.\n- **For each declaration whose name or signature mentions a native\n  numeric type (`UInt64`, `UInt32`, `UInt8`, `Float`, `Fin n`,\n  ...), confirm the body uses native arithmetic on that type.** A\n  body that converts to `Nat`/`Int`, runs the Nat-level algorithm,\n  and converts back is a wrong-shape scaffold even when the\n  input/output contract is satisfied: the asymptotic complexity is\n  bignum, not the native single-word cost the type promises.\n- **Grep the library for the words `scaffold`, `for now`,\n  `eventual`, `placeholder`, `Phase 1`, `bridge for` in `*.lean`\n  and `ffi/*.c` files.** Every hit is a candidate violation; flag\n  in the review issue.\n- **Does each data-level `def` body run at the canonical fast\n  complexity for its operation?** [PLAN/Phase1.md](Phase1.md) forbids\n  \"alternative implementations with the wrong algorithmic\n  complexity\"; this question makes the rule enforceable at review\n  time. Per [SPEC/design-principles.md §7](../SPEC/design-principles.md),\n  if the library SPEC names an algorithm for the operation, the body\n  must implement it; if the SPEC is silent on performance, the body\n  must still be optimal for that operation's standard problem. Flag\n  linear-time `pow` where square-and-multiply is canonical, `a^n % p`\n  where Nat-level square-and-multiply is canonical, descending\n  linear-scan `floorSqrt` where Newton is canonical, unmemoised\n  Pascal `binom` where the multiplicative formula is canonical,\n  rebuild-from-scratch where an incremental update is canonical.\n  Same remedy as wrong-shape scaffolds: fix the body, or delete the\n  declaration.\n- **Are there one-line aliases of an adjacent declaration, lemmas\n  duplicating Lean core, or names that misrepresent the body?**\n  Flag any `def`/`theorem` whose body is `exact <other-decl>` for\n  an `<other-decl>` defined in the same file (it should be deleted\n  and call sites pointed at the real declaration). Flag any local\n  `Nat`/`Int`/`UInt64`/`Array`/`List` lemma whose statement\n  matches a same-shape declaration in `Init.*` or the Lean stdlib\n  (it should be deleted and call sites pointed at the core lemma).\n  Flag any name whose verb or qualifier doesn't appear anywhere in\n  the body (e.g. a lemma named `foo_sub_bar` whose statement\n  contains no subtraction).\n\n## Output\n\nGitHub issues flagging gaps. This may take multiple sessions per library.\n\n## Exit criteria\n\nFor library `hex-foo`, Phase 2 is done when a reviewer *agent* (not\nthe author of the scaffolding) has read the scaffolded code against\n`SPEC/Libraries/hex-foo.md`, opened follow-up issues for any gaps it\nidentifies, and committed a machine-checkable token file\n`status/hex-foo.scaffolding-reviewed` recording that the review has\nbeen performed.\n\nRecord completion by bumping `libraries.yml[L].done_through` to `2`.\nThe `status/hex-foo.scaffolding-reviewed` token is the separate\n*point-in-time attestation* of the review; `libraries.yml` is the\nmutable phase counter. Both are required at Phase 2 exit.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/30ed5d58-e06e-47c0-9b93-a54b68002c06.stdout:49:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_26","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' SPEC/Libraries/hex-gfq-ring.md\"","aggregated_output":"# hex-gfq-ring (GF(q) as a ring, depends on hex-poly-fp)\n\nCanonical quotient-ring implementation for `F_p[x] / (f)`.\n\nWe only form quotient rings by a nonconstant modulus. Concretely, the\npublic quotient type should carry a hypothesis `0 < f.degree`, so the\ncanonical-representative invariant \"degree < deg(f)\" is meaningful and\nwe do not need to special-case `f = 0` or constant moduli throughout the\nAPI.\n\n**Contents:**\n- `reduceMod (f : FpPoly p) : FpPoly p → FpPoly p` — canonical remainder\n  on division by `f`\n- `PolyQuotient p f hf` — quotient elements, represented by a reduced\n  polynomial modulo `f`\n- Smart constructor `ofPoly : FpPoly p → PolyQuotient p f hf`\n- Projection `repr : PolyQuotient p f hf → FpPoly p`\n- Ring operations: addition, multiplication, negation, subtraction,\n  exponentiation; every operation reduces via `reduceMod`.\n  `pow x n` is square-and-multiply (`O(log n)` quotient-ring\n  multiplications). `nsmul n x` and `natCast n` use binary\n  decomposition with the same complexity; the textbook\n  `n+1 ↦ pred + 1` recursion is forbidden.\n- `Lean.Grind.CommRing (PolyQuotient p f hf)` instance\n\nRepresentation choice: the stored representative is always canonical.\nCallers do not manage reduction manually; `ofPoly` and all ring\noperations normalize through `reduceMod`. Equality of quotient elements\nis therefore equality of canonical representatives, not a separate\nsetoid-style relation.\n\nThis does NOT require `f` to be irreducible — the quotient is always a\nring. When `f` is irreducible, the same underlying representation\nsupports a field structure; that extension belongs to hex-gfq-field.\n\n**Key properties:**\n- `repr (ofPoly a) = reduceMod f a`\n- `degree (repr x) < degree f`\n- `ofPoly (reduceMod f a) = ofPoly a`\n- `reduceMod f (a + b) = reduceMod f (reduceMod f a + reduceMod f b)`\n- `reduceMod f (a * b) = reduceMod f (reduceMod f a * reduceMod f b)`\n- Ring axioms for `PolyQuotient p f hf`\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/30ed5d58-e06e-47c0-9b93-a54b68002c06.stdout:61:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_32","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' HexModArith/Ring.lean\"","aggregated_output":"import Init.Grind.Ring.Basic\nimport HexModArith.Basic\n\n/-!\nRing-facing `ZMod64` API for `hex-mod-arith`.\n\nThis module adds the negation/cast surface around the executable `ZMod64`\noperations and exposes the `Lean.Grind` semiring/ring/commutative-ring\ninstances expected by downstream libraries.\n-/\nnamespace Hex\n\nnamespace ZMod64\n\nvariable {p : Nat} [Bounds p]\n\n/-- The additive inverse represented by the complementary residue mod `p`. -/\ndef neg (a : ZMod64 p) : ZMod64 p := by\n  by_cases hp : p = UInt64.word\n  · refine ⟨-a.val, ?_⟩\n    simpa [hp] using (UInt64.toNat_lt_size (-a.val))\n  · have hpLt : p < UInt64.word := Nat.lt_of_le_of_ne (Bounds.pLeR (p := p)) hp\n    let c64 := complementWord p hpLt\n    by_cases hzero : a.val = 0\n    · refine ⟨0, ?_⟩\n      simp [Bounds.pPos (p := p)]\n    · exact ⟨-a.val - c64, by sorry⟩\n\n/-- Natural-number literals in `ZMod64`. -/\ndef natCast (p : Nat) [Bounds p] (n : Nat) : ZMod64 p :=\n  ofNat p n\n\n/-- Natural scalar multiplication on `ZMod64`. -/\ndef nsmul (n : Nat) (a : ZMod64 p) : ZMod64 p :=\n  ofNat p (n * a.toNat)\n\n/-- Integer literals in `ZMod64`, reduced mod `p`. -/\ndef intCast (p : Nat) [Bounds p] : Int → ZMod64 p\n  | .ofNat n => natCast p n\n  | .negSucc n => neg (natCast p (n + 1))\n\n/-- Integer scalar multiplication on `ZMod64`. -/\ndef zsmul (i : Int) (a : ZMod64 p) : ZMod64 p :=\n  match i with\n  | .ofNat n => nsmul n a\n  | .negSucc n => neg (nsmul (n + 1) a)\n\ninstance : Neg (ZMod64 p) where\n  neg := neg\n\ninstance : NatCast (ZMod64 p) where\n  natCast := natCast p\n\ninstance (n : Nat) : OfNat (ZMod64 p) n where\n  ofNat := natCast p n\n\ninstance : SMul Nat (ZMod64 p) where\n  smul := nsmul\n\ninstance : IntCast (ZMod64 p) where\n  intCast := intCast p\n\ninstance : SMul Int (ZMod64 p) where\n  smul := zsmul\n\n@[simp] theorem toNat_natCast (n : Nat) : (natCast p n).toNat = n % p := by\n  rw [natCast, toNat_ofNat]\n\n@[simp] theorem toNat_neg (a : ZMod64 p) : (neg a).toNat = (p - a.toNat) % p := by\n  sorry\n\n@[simp] theorem toNat_nsmul (n : Nat) (a : ZMod64 p) :\n    (nsmul n a).toNat = (n * a.toNat) % p := by\n  rw [nsmul, toNat_ofNat]\n\n/-- Nat casts agree exactly when their representatives are congruent mod `p`. -/\ntheorem natCast_eq_natCast_iff (x y : Nat) :\n    ((x : ZMod64 p) = y) ↔ x % p = y % p := by\n  constructor\n  · intro h\n    simpa using congrArg ZMod64.toNat h\n  · intro h\n    apply ext\n    apply UInt64.toNat_inj.mp\n    simpa [toNat_natCast] using h\n\n/-- A Nat literal vanishes in `ZMod64 p` exactly when `p` divides it. -/\ntheorem natCast_eq_zero_iff_dvd (n : Nat) : ((n : ZMod64 p) = 0) ↔ p ∣ n := by\n  constructor\n  · intro h\n    exact Nat.dvd_of_mod_eq_zero ((natCast_eq_natCast_iff (p := p) n 0).mp h)\n  · intro h\n    exact (natCast_eq_natCast_iff (p := p) n 0).2 (Nat.mod_eq_zero_of_dvd h)\n\n@[simp] theorem natCast_self : ((p : Nat) : ZMod64 p) = 0 := by\n  exact (natCast_eq_natCast_iff (p := p) p 0).2 (by simp)\n\n/-- The spec-level inverse law on canonical representatives. -/\ntheorem toNat_inv (a : ZMod64 p) (hcop : Nat.Coprime a.val.toNat p) :\n    (a.inv * a).toNat = 1 % p := by\n  simpa [ZMod64.toNat_eq_val] using inv_mul_eq_one (p := p) a hcop\n\ninstance : Lean.Grind.Semiring (ZMod64 p) := by\n  refine Lean.Grind.Semiring.mk ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_\n  · intro a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a b\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a b c\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a b c\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a b c\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a b c\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a n\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro n\n    sorry\n  · intro n\n    sorry\n  · intro n a\n    sorry\n\ninstance : Lean.Grind.Ring (ZMod64 p) := by\n  refine Lean.Grind.Ring.mk ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_\n  · intro a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro a b\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro i a\n    ext\n    sorry\n  · intro n a\n    sorry\n  · intro n\n    sorry\n  · intro i\n    sorry\n\ninstance : Lean.Grind.CommRing (ZMod64 p) := by\n  refine Lean.Grind.CommRing.mk ?_\n  intro a b\n  ext\n  sorry\n\ninstance : Lean.Grind.IsCharP (ZMod64 p) p where\n  ofNat_ext_iff {x y} := natCast_eq_natCast_iff (p := p) x y\n\nend ZMod64\n\nend Hex\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

./sessions/30ed5d58-e06e-47c0-9b93-a54b68002c06.stdout:80:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_43","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"git diff -- .claude/CLAUDE.md | sed -n '1,140p'\"","aggregated_output":"diff --git a/.claude/CLAUDE.md b/.claude/CLAUDE.md\nindex 5628d19..6234bee 100644\n--- a/.claude/CLAUDE.md\n+++ b/.claude/CLAUDE.md\n@@ -29,3 +29,70 @@ Commits made during the turn should mention the progress file.\n Check diagnostics after every step; don't continue past errors. Build\n via `lake build`, not `lean` directly. `native_decide` is banned (see\n SPEC).\n+\n+<!-- pod-managed-codex-agents -->\n+\n+# Project .claude/CLAUDE.md\n+\n+# hex — agent-specific conventions\n+\n+Conventions specifically for LLM agents working on this project.\n+General project doctrine (Mathlib-free split, SPEC/PLAN structure,\n+key files) lives in `SPEC/` and `PLAN.md`; start there for\n+orientation.\n+\n+## Style\n+\n+Don't add \"research completed\" timestamps, progress notes, or\n+meta-commentary about the history of our research process to any\n+file. The git history tracks that. SPEC files and `PLAN/` contain\n+the current state of the design, not a journal of how we got there.\n+\n+## Per-turn progress files\n+\n+Start of turn: read the most recent file in `progress/` (ISO-8601\n+timestamps sort chronologically). If only `progress/0000-init.md`\n+exists, the repo is freshly initialised — proceed with Phase 0.\n+\n+End of turn: write `progress/<UTC-timestamp>.md` with sections\n+**Accomplished** / **Current frontier** / **Next step** / **Blockers**.\n+Scope these to *your* session — what you touched, where you stopped,\n+what you think comes next for your corner of the project.\n+Commits made during the turn should mention the progress file.\n+\n+## Lean\n+\n+Check diagnostics after every step; don't continue past errors. Build\n+via `lake build`, not `lean` directly. `native_decide` is banned (see\n+SPEC).\n+\n+# Pod Codex Guidance\n+\n+# Pod Agent Session\n+\n+You are running as an autonomous agent launched by `pod`. This is a\n+non-interactive session via `codex exec` — there is no human to answer\n+questions. Never ask for confirmation or approval. Just do the work.\n+\n+Each agent runs in its own git worktree on its own branch, coordinating\n+via GitHub issues, labels, and PRs. The `coordination` script is already\n+on your PATH — just run it directly (e.g. `coordination orient`,\n+`coordination claim 42`). Do NOT search for it or try to locate it.\n+\n+Session UUID is available as `$POD_SESSION_ID`.\n+\n+## Agent Types\n+\n+- **Planners**: create work items as GitHub issues, then exit\n+- **Workers**: claim and execute issues using the `agent-worker-flow` skill\n+- **Repair**: salvage unhealthy PRs (merge conflicts, failed CI, stuck CI).\n+  Two outcomes only: salvaged or abandoned via\n+  `coordination close-pr-unsalvageable`. No escalation to humans.\n+\n+See the `agent-worker-flow` skill (for workers) or the `/repair` command\n+(for repair sessions) for the full workflow.\n+\n+## Off-limits Files\n+\n+Agents must not modify the project's top-level AGENTS.md or roadmap file\n+(`PLAN.md`). PRs touching these files are rejected by `coordination create-pr`.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

./sessions/0f8dfb2d-cc42-4ff8-9cea-e6c824989f3b.stdout:5:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_1","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' /Users/kim/projects/lean/hex/worktrees/0f8dfb2d/.pod-codex-home/skills/agent-worker-flow/SKILL.md\"","aggregated_output":"---\nname: agent-worker-flow\ndescription: Standard claim/branch/verify/publish workflow for pod agent sessions. Read this skill at the start of any feature, review, summarize, or meditate session.\nallowed-tools: Bash, Read, Glob, Grep\n---\n\n# Standard Worker Flow for Pod Agent Sessions\n\nThis skill covers the shared workflow used by all pod worker agents.\nSession-specific commands reference this skill rather than duplicating it.\n\n## Coordination Reference\n\nThe `coordination` script handles all GitHub-based multi-agent coordination.\nSession UUID is available as `$POD_SESSION_ID` (exported by `pod`).\nThe `gh` CLI defaults to the current repo, so `--repo` is not needed.\n\n| Command | What it does |\n|---------|-------------|\n| `coordination orient` | List unclaimed/claimed issues, open PRs, PRs needing attention |\n| `coordination plan [--label L] \"title\"` | Create GitHub issue with agent-plan + optional label; body from stdin |\n| `coordination create-pr N [--partial] [\"title\"]` | Push branch, create PR closing issue #N, enable auto-merge, swap `claimed` → `has-pr`. With `--partial`: adds `replan` label. |\n| `coordination claim-fix N` | Comment on failing PR #N claiming fix (30min cooldown) |\n| `coordination close-pr N \"reason\"` | Comment reason and close PR #N |\n| `coordination list-unclaimed [--label L]` | List unclaimed agent-plan issues (FIFO order); optional label filter |\n| `coordination queue-depth [L]` | Count of unclaimed issues; optional label for per-type count |\n| `coordination claim N` | Claim issue #N — adds `claimed` label + comment, detects races |\n| `coordination skip N \"reason\"` | Mark claimed issue as needing replan — removes `claimed`, adds `replan` label |\n| `coordination add-dep N M` | Add `depends-on: #M` to issue #N's body; adds `blocked` label if #M is open |\n| `coordination check-blocked` | Unblock issues whose `depends-on` dependencies are all closed |\n| `coordination release-stale-claims [SECS]` | Release claimed issues with no PR after SECS seconds (default 4h); **manual use only** |\n| `coordination lock-planner` | Acquire advisory planner lock (20min TTL) |\n| `coordination unlock-planner` | Release planner lock early |\n| `coordination critical-path-depth [L]` | Count unclaimed critical-path issues; optional label filter |\n| `coordination set-target N` | Planner sets recommended target agent count (wind-down use only) |\n\n**Issue lifecycle**: planner creates issue (label: `agent-plan`) →\nworker claims it (adds label: `claimed`) → worker creates PR closing it\n(label swaps to `has-pr`) → auto-merge squash-merges.\nIssues marked `replan` (by skip, partial completion, or worker-led\ndecomposition) are handled by the next planner. Issues with `has-pr` are\nexcluded from `list-unclaimed` and `queue-depth`.\n\n**Partial completion**: worker uses `--partial` → label swaps to\n`replan`. A planner creates a new issue for remaining work, then closes\nthe `replan` issue with a link to the new one.\n\n**Worker-led decomposition**: if the claimed issue is too large for one\nsession, worker creates sub-issues and `coordination skip`s the parent\nwith a `Decomposed into #X, #Y` breadcrumb comment. The next planner\neither closes the parent (sub-issues fully cover it) or narrows it to the\nresidual scope. See \"Assess Scope\" (Step 4b) for the full procedure.\n\n**Dependencies**: Issues can declare `depends-on: #N` in their body.\n`coordination plan` auto-adds the `blocked` label if any dependency is\nopen. `check-blocked` (run by `pod` each loop) removes `blocked` when\nall dependencies close. Blocked issues are excluded from\n`list-unclaimed` and `queue-depth`.\n\n**Branch naming**: `agent/<first-8-chars-of-UUID>`\n**Plan files**: `plans/<UUID-prefix>.md`\n**Progress files**: `progress/<UTC-timestamp>_<UUID-prefix>.md`\n\n## Step 1: Claim a Work Item\n\n```\ncoordination orient\n```\n\n**Priority order:**\n0. **Human oversight directives first**: Check for open `human-oversight` issues before\n   anything else. These are direct instructions from the project owner and take absolute\n   precedence over all other work:\n   ```\n   coordination list-unclaimed --label human-oversight\n   ```\n   If any are open and unclaimed, claim the oldest one immediately.\n   **These issues cannot be skipped or refused because you disagree with the approach.**\n   The only valid exit from a `human-oversight` issue is completing it, or posting a\n   comment explaining a genuine technical blocker (e.g. a missing dependency), then\n   using `coordination skip` with that reason. Do not `skip` because you think a\n   different approach is better — that is the owner's call, not yours.\n1. **Oldest unclaimed issue** of your type:\n   ```\n   coordination list-unclaimed --label <your-label>\n   ```\n\n**Don't repair PRs from a worker session.** PR health (merge conflicts,\nfailed CI, stuck CI) is the `repair` agent's responsibility; pod dispatches\n`/repair` automatically when `coordination list-pr-repair` reports\ncandidates, ahead of `/plan` / `/work`. Focus on fresh issue work.\n\nIf the queue is empty, write a brief progress note and exit.\n\n```\ncoordination claim <issue-number>\n```\n\n**You MUST check the output.** If it says `CLAIM FAILED`, you MUST NOT work\non that issue — pick a different one. Only proceed if the output says\n`Claimed issue #N`. Read the full issue body:\n```\ngh issue view <N> --json body --jq .body\n```\n\n## Step 2: Set Up\n\n```bash\ngit checkout -b agent/<first-8-chars-of-session-UUID>\ngit rev-parse HEAD      # record starting commit\n```\n\n**If the branch already exists** (common in reused worktrees): check for an\nopen PR on it first (`gh pr list --head agent/<id>`). If a PR exists, create\na new branch with a suffix (`agent/<id>-v2`). If no PR exists, reset it to\nmaster: `git checkout agent/<id> && git reset --hard origin/master`.\n\nRecord any project-specific quality metrics (e.g. sorry count, test coverage)\nas described in the project's CLAUDE.md.\n\n## Step 3: Codebase Orientation\n\nRead the specific files mentioned in the plan/issue. Understand the current state\nof code you'll be modifying. Don't read progress history — the issue body provides\nthat context.\n\n## Step 4: Verify Assumptions\n\nCheck that the plan's assumptions still hold:\n- Quality metrics match what the issue says\n- Files mentioned in the issue still exist and haven't been restructured\n- No recently merged PR invalidates the plan\n\nIf stale:\n```\ncoordination skip <issue-number> \"reason: <what changed>\"\n```\nGo back to Step 1 and try the next issue.\n\n**PR fix plans**: If the plan asks you to fix a broken PR, use judgement. If the\nPR is low quality or not worth salvaging:\n```\ncoordination close-pr <pr-number> \"reason: <why not worth fixing>\"\n```\n\n## Step 4b: Assess Scope\n\nAfter orienting but **before writing code**, check whether the task fits\nin a single session. Warning signs it doesn't:\n\n- Target file is 500+ lines and you need to understand most of it\n- The work naturally splits into independent sub-lemmas or sub-tasks\n- Difficulty feels higher than the issue says\n\nIf the issue is too large, **decomposing it into smaller sub-issues is a\nnormal success path**, not a failure mode. You have the freshest codebase\ncontext and can usually scope sub-tasks more accurately than a planner could\nin advance. A good decomposition is more valuable than a failed heroic\nattempt — and far better than overrunning the session trying to salvage it.\n\nYou may decompose when any of these is true:\n- the claimed issue is too large for one session,\n- the work naturally splits into independent sub-tasks,\n- you can write self-contained successor issues without further investigation.\n\n```bash\n# 1. Create self-contained sub-issues. Use `coordination plan` exactly as a\n#    planner would — same body template (Current state / Deliverables /\n#    Context / Verification), same label. Note: `coordination plan` does\n#    only best-effort title-keyword overlap warnings; it does not hold the\n#    planner lock and cannot atomically dedupe against concurrent creators.\n#    If you see open issues that look related, link or coordinate\n#    explicitly in the sub-issue body rather than relying on the warning.\necho \"body...\" | coordination plan --label feature \"Sub-task 1: ...\"\necho \"body...\" | coordination plan --label feature \"Sub-task 2: ...\"\n\n# 2. Link ordering dependencies if any sub-task must precede another.\n#    Do NOT add `depends-on: #<parent>` — the parent is about to be\n#    superseded; depend on real predecessor sub-issues instead.\ncoordination add-dep <sub2> <sub1>\n\n# 3. Leave a machine-readable breadcrumb on the parent. The planner's\n#    replan-triage step keys off this exact `Decomposed into #X, #Y`\n#    phrasing — keep it on a single line at the start of the comment.\ngh issue comment <parent> --body \"Decomposed into #<sub1>, #<sub2>\n\n(reason: <one-line scope assessment>)\"\n\n# 4. Release the claim by marking the parent for planner triage. This\n#    routes through pod's claim-state machinery and clears the in-process\n#    `state.claimed_issue` correctly. Do NOT use `gh issue close` directly:\n#    it leaves pod's session-end cleanup thinking the issue is still\n#    claimed and will attempt a stray `coordination skip` on a closed\n#    issue at exit.\ncoordination skip <parent> \"Decomposed into #<sub1>, #<sub2> — see comment\"\n```\n\nThe planner's next replan-triage cycle picks the parent up and either\ncloses it (if the sub-issues fully cover it) or narrows the body to the\nresidual scope (if not).\n\nAfter decomposing, you have two options:\n\n1. **Continue on one of the sub-issues**: claim it via `coordination claim`,\n   then return to Step 2 with the sub-issue. Common case when the parent\n   was just two work items glued together.\n2. **Stop and exit**: if you've used most of your session orienting, write a\n   brief progress entry and exit. The next worker will claim a sub-issue.\n\nIf you've already done a coherent subset of the parent's work *before*\ndeciding to decompose, prefer the partial-PR path:\n\n```bash\n# Steps 1-3 above (create sub-issues for the remaining work, leave the\n# `Decomposed into #X, #Y` breadcrumb on the parent).\n\n# Then land your coherent subset. `--partial` marks the parent `replan`.\ncoordination create-pr <parent> --partial \"feat: <what landed>\"\n```\n\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/0f8dfb2d-cc42-4ff8-9cea-e6c824989f3b.stdout:9:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_3","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc 'coordination list-unclaimed'","aggregated_output":"#2152 HexPolyZ: finish quotient-by-gcd Rat square-free lemma (2026-05-05T00:56)\n#2157 HexPolyFp: repair Yun derivative-active contribution invariant (2026-05-05T01:00)\n#2161 HexHensel: prove quadratic multifactor product spec (2026-05-05T01:04)\n#2163 HexGF2: prove packedReduceWord reduced-remainder representation (2026-05-05T01:05)\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/0f8dfb2d-cc42-4ff8-9cea-e6c824989f3b.stdout:21:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_11","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"gh issue view 2163 --json number,title,labels,body --jq '{number,title,labels:[.labels[].name],body}'\"","aggregated_output":"{\"body\":\"**Current state**\\n\\nIssue #2158 asks for the final packed inverse bridge, but the key missing proof is a reusable representation lemma: when a polynomial remainder modulo `GF2Poly.ofUInt64Monic irr n` is reduced to degree `\\u003c n \\u003c 64`, `GF2Poly.packedReduceWord n irr p` repackages the low stored word back to that reduced polynomial.\\n\\nThe final bridge currently has to connect:\\n\\n```lean\\nGF2Poly.ofUInt64 (GF2Poly.canonicalWordLT n hn64 (GF2Poly.packedInvWord n irr w))\\n```\\n\\nwith the polynomial remainder:\\n\\n```lean\\n(GF2Poly.xgcd (GF2Poly.ofUInt64 w) (GF2Poly.ofUInt64Monic irr n)).left %\\n  GF2Poly.ofUInt64Monic irr n\\n```\\n\\nThat should be proved via a local representation lemma for `packedReduceWord`, not by expanding CLMUL or xgcd.\\n\\n**Deliverables**\\n\\n1. In `HexGF2/Euclid.lean`, add the reusable helper lemmas needed to prove that a reduced polynomial remainder of degree `\\u003c n \\u003c 64` is equal to the single-word polynomial obtained from `packedReduceWord n irr p`.\\n2. Include the needed `lowerMask`/canonical-word bit facts locally, keeping the proof surface generic and reusable by the final inverse theorem.\\n3. Do not change executable definitions for `lowerMask`, `packedReduceWord`, `canonicalWordLT`, `packedInvWord`, `xgcd`, or polynomial arithmetic.\\n\\n**Context**\\n\\n- Decomposed from #2158.\\n- Ready pair: `HexGF2 -\\u003e Phase 5`.\\n- Primary file: `HexGF2/Euclid.lean`.\\n- Relevant existing definitions: `lowerMask`, `packedReduceWord`, `canonicalWordLT`, `ofUInt64Monic`, `packedInvWord`.\\n- Relevant existing proof APIs: `GF2Poly.ext_coeff`, `coeff_eq_false_of_degree?_lt`, `degree?_isSome_of_isZero_false`, `ofUInt64_mul_ofUInt64`.\\n\\n**Verification**\\n\\n- `lake build HexGF2.Euclid`\\n- `lake build HexGF2`\\n- `python3 scripts/status.py HexGF2`\\n- `python3 scripts/check_dag.py`\\n- `git diff --check`\\n- Scan touched files for forbidden placeholders: no `axiom`, no `native_decide`, no `TODO`/`FIXME`, and no new `sorry`.\\n\\n**Out of scope**\\n\\n- Do not prove `GF2Poly.packedReduceWord_clmul_packedInvWord_eq_one`; that is the dependent follow-up.\\n- Do not modify `HexGF2/Field.lean` unless a representation helper needs a narrowly stronger exported theorem statement.\\n- Do not modify top-level `PLAN.md`, top-level `AGENTS.md`, or any `SPEC/` file.\",\"labels\":[\"agent-plan\",\"feature\"],\"number\":2163,\"title\":\"HexGF2: prove packedReduceWord reduced-remainder representation\"}\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

./sessions/0f8dfb2d-cc42-4ff8-9cea-e6c824989f3b.stdout:39:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_20","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc 'git diff -- .claude/CLAUDE.md'","aggregated_output":"diff --git a/.claude/CLAUDE.md b/.claude/CLAUDE.md\nindex 5628d19..6234bee 100644\n--- a/.claude/CLAUDE.md\n+++ b/.claude/CLAUDE.md\n@@ -29,3 +29,70 @@ Commits made during the turn should mention the progress file.\n Check diagnostics after every step; don't continue past errors. Build\n via `lake build`, not `lean` directly. `native_decide` is banned (see\n SPEC).\n+\n+<!-- pod-managed-codex-agents -->\n+\n+# Project .claude/CLAUDE.md\n+\n+# hex — agent-specific conventions\n+\n+Conventions specifically for LLM agents working on this project.\n+General project doctrine (Mathlib-free split, SPEC/PLAN structure,\n+key files) lives in `SPEC/` and `PLAN.md`; start there for\n+orientation.\n+\n+## Style\n+\n+Don't add \"research completed\" timestamps, progress notes, or\n+meta-commentary about the history of our research process to any\n+file. The git history tracks that. SPEC files and `PLAN/` contain\n+the current state of the design, not a journal of how we got there.\n+\n+## Per-turn progress files\n+\n+Start of turn: read the most recent file in `progress/` (ISO-8601\n+timestamps sort chronologically). If only `progress/0000-init.md`\n+exists, the repo is freshly initialised — proceed with Phase 0.\n+\n+End of turn: write `progress/<UTC-timestamp>.md` with sections\n+**Accomplished** / **Current frontier** / **Next step** / **Blockers**.\n+Scope these to *your* session — what you touched, where you stopped,\n+what you think comes next for your corner of the project.\n+Commits made during the turn should mention the progress file.\n+\n+## Lean\n+\n+Check diagnostics after every step; don't continue past errors. Build\n+via `lake build`, not `lean` directly. `native_decide` is banned (see\n+SPEC).\n+\n+# Pod Codex Guidance\n+\n+# Pod Agent Session\n+\n+You are running as an autonomous agent launched by `pod`. This is a\n+non-interactive session via `codex exec` — there is no human to answer\n+questions. Never ask for confirmation or approval. Just do the work.\n+\n+Each agent runs in its own git worktree on its own branch, coordinating\n+via GitHub issues, labels, and PRs. The `coordination` script is already\n+on your PATH — just run it directly (e.g. `coordination orient`,\n+`coordination claim 42`). Do NOT search for it or try to locate it.\n+\n+Session UUID is available as `$POD_SESSION_ID`.\n+\n+## Agent Types\n+\n+- **Planners**: create work items as GitHub issues, then exit\n+- **Workers**: claim and execute issues using the `agent-worker-flow` skill\n+- **Repair**: salvage unhealthy PRs (merge conflicts, failed CI, stuck CI).\n+  Two outcomes only: salvaged or abandoned via\n+  `coordination close-pr-unsalvageable`. No escalation to humans.\n+\n+See the `agent-worker-flow` skill (for workers) or the `/repair` command\n+(for repair sessions) for the full workflow.\n+\n+## Off-limits Files\n+\n+Agents must not modify the project's top-level AGENTS.md or roadmap file\n+(`PLAN.md`). PRs touching these files are rejected by `coordination create-pr`.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/0f8dfb2d-cc42-4ff8-9cea-e6c824989f3b.stdout:50:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_26","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' PLAN/Phase5.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Phase 5: Implementation Work Loop\n\n**Coupling:** local. Library L can start Phase 5 once\n`libraries.yml[L].done_through ≥ 4`. L's own proofs only rely on deps'\ndeclared APIs (from the dep's Phase 1), not on deps' proofs.\n\nFill in `sorry`'d proofs. One GitHub issue per theorem or small group\nof related theorems.\n\n## Task selection\n\nIn priority order:\n1. Items that unblock the most downstream work (high fan-out in the DAG).\n2. Items assessed as mathematically hardest (from Phase 2 review or\n   periodic status checks).\n3. Items with the most `sorry`s in a single file.\n\n## Workflow\n\n- **Work top-down.** Replace `sorry` with structured proof skeletons\n  (tactic blocks with intermediate `sorry`s) before filling in helper\n  lemmas. This reveals the proof structure early.\n\n- **Push sorries earlier.** When a proof is hard, replace it with a proof outline\n  that cites new, clearly-stated lemmas (which may themselves be\n  `sorry`) one level closer to the foundations. See\n  `SPEC/design-principles.md` for the authoritative statement of the\n  idiom.\n\n- **PRs with auto-merge** enabled where CI is passing.\n\n- **Periodic status checks** (every ~50 merged PRs or at natural\n  milestones): regression check for definition-level sorries, identify\n  hardest remaining items, flag neglected libraries.\n\n## Exit criteria\n\nFor library `hex-foo`, Phase 5 is done when it has zero `sorry`\noccurrences (counted across all declarations, not just top-level\ntheorems), `lake build` is green on the pinned toolchain, and the\nPhase 3 conformance suite for the library still passes.\n\nRecord completion by bumping `libraries.yml[L].done_through` to `5`.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

./sessions/10302747-02b0-470f-99eb-f2c018c87a42.stdout:22:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_10","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,240p' progress/20260614T040000Z_c85703f0.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Progress: #6918 — review PR #6917 (explicit-witness resultant divisibility)\n\nReview session. No source changes; findings posted as a PR comment on #6917\n(self-approval is blocked since all agent commits share one account).\n\n## Accomplished\n\nReviewed PR #6917 (closes #6858), which adds three theorems to\n`HexBerlekampZassenhausMathlib/Resultant.lean`:\n\n- `det_dvd_of_cols_dvd` (+ helper `prod_cols_dvd_prod_univ_of_injective`):\n  generalises `det_dvd_of_left_cols_dvd` from the leading `castAdd` block to an\n  arbitrary **injective** column family — `m ^ d ∣ det` when any `d` distinct\n  columns are entrywise `m`-divisible.\n- `commonFactor_dvd_resultant`: instantiates the #6857 Sylvester column\n  reduction from explicit witnesses `f = q*a + C m*r`, `g = q*b + C m*s`, kills\n  the determinant factor `(b.coeff (n-d))^d` to `1` via the pivot hypothesis,\n  and rewrites the Sylvester determinant to `Polynomial.resultant`.\n- `commonFactor_dvd_resultant_of_monic_cofactor`: caller-friendly monic-cofactor\n  form deriving the pivot coefficient from `b.Monic`.\n\n**Verdict: approve.** Verified locally:\n\n- `lake build HexBerlekampZassenhausMathlib.Resultant` succeeds (the Mathlib\n  layer is not CI-built, so this needed a local build on the PR commit after\n  `lake exe cache get`). No `sorry`/`axiom`/`native_decide` in added lines.\n- `scripts/check_dag.py` exit 0; `git diff --check` clean. CI\n  `build`/`conformance`/`build-macos` all green.\n- The composition is sound: `resultant f g` is definitionally the Sylvester\n  determinant (`Resultant.lean:255` `rfl`), so the final rewrite has no\n  sign/unit gap. Stays over `Polynomial ℤ` / `Matrix _ ℤ`; no `ZMod (p^k)`\n  wrapper and no Hensel/CLD dependency (deliverable 2 confirmed).\n\n## Key findings (recorded on the PR)\n\n1. Non-blocking: `hq_monic` and `hq_deg` are non-load-bearing in\n   `commonFactor_dvd_resultant`. `sylvester_commonFactor_colReduce` requires\n   neither monicity nor `q.natDegree = d`; the `m^d` divisibility comes purely\n   from the column-reduction structure and degree bounds. The two\n   `have hq_*_used := hq_*` lines are dead code silencing the unused-binder\n   linter. Recommend dropping the hypotheses or underscore-prefixing the\n   binders and deleting the `_used` haves. The same hypotheses thread unused\n   through the monic-cofactor wrapper, so #6849 will be forced to supply proofs\n   the theorem ignores.\n2. Minor: the new `hpiv_inj` proof (~line 844) uses the deprecated\n   `Fin.coe_natAdd` (build warns → `Fin.val_natAdd`); consistent with several\n   pre-existing occurrences in the file.\n\n## Current frontier\n\nReview complete. PR is green and mergeable.\n\n## Next step\n\n#6849 packages the public map-to-`ZMod (p^k)` wrapper consuming\n`commonFactor_dvd_resultant_of_monic_cofactor`.\n\n## Blockers\n\nNone.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/10302747-02b0-470f-99eb-f2c018c87a42.stdout:23:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_11","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,240p' progress/20260614T020000Z_15d7753f.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Progress: #6857 — Sylvester monic-triangular column transform (partial)\n\n## Accomplished\n\nLanded the complete *mechanism* for the common-factor Sylvester\ncolumn reduction in `HexBerlekampZassenhausMathlib/Resultant.lean`, as\nfour reusable lemmas over a general `CommRing R` with scalar `c`:\n\n- `sylvesterMap_commonFactor_smul` — the `c`-scaled generalization of\n  the exact syzygy `sylvesterMap_commonFactor_syzygy`. From witnesses\n  `f = q*a + C c*r`, `g = q*b + C c*s`, the image of the\n  `(-a X^t, b X^t)` direction is `C c * ((r*b - s*a) * X^t)` (the exact\n  syzygy is the `c = 0` case).\n- `sylvester_mulVec_commonFactor_smul` — multiplying the Sylvester\n  matrix by the coordinate vector of that direction gives entry `i`\n  equal to `c * ((r*b - s*a) * X^t).coeff i`. Routes through\n  `toMatrix_sylvesterMap'` + `LinearMap.toMatrix_mulVec_repr` +\n  `degreeLT.basis_repr`. So the selected column combination is\n  entrywise divisible by `c`.\n- `det_updateCol_mulVec` — general matrix fact: replacing column `p` by\n  `A.mulVec w` multiplies the determinant by the pivot coefficient\n  `w p`. Column-form repackaging of `Matrix.det_updateCol_sum`.\n- `sylvester_commonFactor_colReduceStep` — capstone: the single column\n  step. Replacing a Sylvester column by the syzygy combination scales\n  `det` by the pivot coefficient `w p` and yields a column divisible by\n  `c`. Det-preserving exactly when `w p = 1`.\n\n## Key finding (premise refinement for the assembled `A'`)\n\nThe literal deliverable `A'.det = sylvester.det` with `d` divisible\ncolumns is achievable, but **only when each pivot coefficient is a\nunit**. The pivot coefficient `w p` at the natural pivot (top cofactor\nshift column, position `(n-d)+t` on the `f`-shift side) is the cofactor\nleading coefficient `b.coeff (n-d) = leadingCoeff b`. With a *monic*\ncofactor it is `1` and the step is determinant-preserving — this is\nexactly the \"monic triangular\" structure the issue title names.\n\nWithout the monic/unit hypothesis the step instead scales `det` by\n`(leadingCoeff b)^d`, so the honest general statement is\n`A'.det = (leadingCoeff g)^d * sylvester.det` (given `q.Monic`,\n`leadingCoeff g = leadingCoeff b`). Either form serves #6858: the monic\nform gives `A'.det = sylvester.det` directly; the general form needs\n`#6858` to clear the `(leadingCoeff g)^d` factor by coprimality of `c`\nwith the leading coefficient (the `p ∤ lc` primitivity already present\nin the BZ setting). This is the \"genuinely required side condition\" the\nissue invited; it is not an unsoundness — the transform exists either\nway.\n\n## Current frontier\n\nThe single-column step is complete and verified. Remaining: assemble it\nover the `d = q.natDegree` shifts `t : Fin d` into the matrix `A'`.\n\n## Next step (assembly recipe)\n\n1. Set `m = f.natDegree`, `n = g.natDegree`, `d = q.natDegree`.\n2. Pivots `p_t = (⟨(n-d)+t, _⟩ : Fin n).natAdd m` (top `d` `f`-shift\n   columns). The `w_t`-coordinate at `p_t` is `b.coeff (n-d)`; under the\n   monic-cofactor side condition this is `1`.\n3. Iterate `sylvester_commonFactor_colReduceStep` over `t` in\n   **decreasing** order. The direction `w_t` is supported (on the\n   `f`-shift block) at positions `t .. t+(n-d)`, so it references pivots\n   `p_{t'}` only for `t' ≤ t`; processing decreasing `t` keeps every\n   referenced column unreplaced, so each combination stays the clean\n   `c`-divisible vector.\n4. Each step preserves `det` (unit pivot) and adds one `c`-divisible\n   column; after `d` steps `A'.det = sylvester.det` with `d` columns\n   divisible by `c`.\n5. `#6858` then feeds `A'` to `det_dvd_of_left_cols_dvd` (after a column\n   reindex moving the divisible block to the left) and rewrites the\n   Sylvester determinant to `resultant`.\n\n## Verification\n\n- `lake build HexBerlekampZassenhausMathlib.Resultant` succeeds.\n- `python3 scripts/check_dag.py` exits 0.\n- No `sorry`/`axiom`/`native_decide`/`TODO`/`FIXME` on added lines.\n\n## Blockers\n\nNone. The assembly is mechanical given the step; the only design choice\nis whether to carry the monic-cofactor side condition (clean\n`A'.det = sylvester.det`) or the general `(leadingCoeff g)^d` factor.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
./sessions/10302747-02b0-470f-99eb-f2c018c87a42.stdout:27:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_13","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' PLAN.md\"","aggregated_output":"# hex — Workflow Plan\n\nYou are a planner agent. Your job is to **survey project state and\ndispatch GitHub issues** for worker agents to pick up. You do not\nimplement — workers do.\n\n## Start of cycle\n\n1. Merge all mergeable + green open PRs before creating new work.\n   Details in [PLAN/Conventions.md](PLAN/Conventions.md).\n2. List current open GitHub issues. These are your ground truth for\n   work already in flight; you will cross-reference against the\n   status survey below to avoid duplicate dispatch.\n\n## Bootstrap check\n\nIf `scripts/status.py` does not yet exist, the repo is pre-bootstrap.\nGo straight to [PLAN/Phase0.md](PLAN/Phase0.md) — Phase 0 is a single\ncritical-path issue handled by one worker. Dispatch (or execute) it\nand wait for it to land before returning here.\n\n## Survey and dispatch\n\nOtherwise, run `scripts/status.py`. It lists every `(library, phase)`\npair that is currently ready for work, and for each pair names:\n\n- the **SPEC file**: `SPEC/Libraries/hex-foo.md` — the library's design;\n- the **PLAN file**: `PLAN/PhaseK.md` — the phase rules and exit criteria;\n- the `libraries.yml` bump expected when the phase completes.\n\nMultiple pairs are ready at once — that is the point of the\nphase-dependency rules. Dispatch in parallel rather than serialising.\nFor each ready pair, decide whether to create new issues using:\n\n- **Existing open-issue coverage.** If open issues already cover a\n  pair adequately, skip it this cycle.\n- **Worker capacity headroom.** Don't dispatch more than workers can\n  reasonably pick up before the next planner cycle.\n\nFor each pair you do choose to dispatch:\n\n1. Read `SPEC/Libraries/hex-foo.md` for the library's design (and\n   `SPEC/SPEC.md` + any cross-cutting SPEC files it links — e.g.\n   `SPEC/testing.md` for Phase 3 — if you haven't already this session).\n2. Read `PLAN/PhaseK.md` for the phase's scope and exit criteria.\n3. **Add more narrow GitHub issues** advancing the pair's work. You\n   are not writing a complete decomposition for the `(library,\n   phase)` pair in one go — you are adding the next batch of work\n   units for workers to pick up, on top of whatever issues already\n   exist. Follow the issue-creation rules in\n   [PLAN/Conventions.md](PLAN/Conventions.md) (canonical body\n   shape, narrow scope, `depends-on:` dependency syntax).\n\n## Once per session\n\nRead [PLAN/Conventions.md](PLAN/Conventions.md) for conventions you\nshouldn't rediscover each time: naming, FFI, PR auto-merge, per-turn\nprogress files, `SPEC/` immutability, the phase-dependency rule\ntable, and how `libraries.yml` relates to `status/` tokens and\nGitHub issues.\n\n## Key files\n\n- [SPEC/SPEC.md](SPEC/SPEC.md) and [SPEC/Libraries/](SPEC/Libraries/)\n  — *what* to build.\n- [libraries.yml](libraries.yml) — per-library phase counter.\n- [PLAN/](PLAN/) — *how* to execute each phase; immutable reference.\n- [PLAN/Releases.md](PLAN/Releases.md) — release readiness predicate.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

./sessions/10302747-02b0-470f-99eb-f2c018c87a42.stdout:36:{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_18","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,260p' PLAN/Conventions.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Conventions\n\nCross-phase conventions that apply across the entire project. Read\nonce per session.\n\n---\n\n## Hard rules\n\n### SPEC immutability\n\n**Do not modify files under `SPEC/`.** The only exception is the\n\"scope of autonomous SPEC edits\" rule in `SPEC/design-principles.md`:\nagents may fix SPEC clauses that are internally contradictory or\nmathematically impossible, with rationale in the PR description.\n\n### Reports do not override SPEC\n\nFiles under `reports/` are research outputs, not policy. When a\nreport identifies a contradiction between existing code and SPEC,\nthe agent acts directly: roll back the contradicting code. The\ncontradiction is evidence that someone wrote code outside the\nSPEC; aligning the code is the default. Editing SPEC is reserved\nfor the narrow §SPEC immutability case (internal contradiction or\nmathematical impossibility) and is not the path to reach for when\na report finds existing code that the SPEC does not sanction.\n\n### Placeholders are for proofs only\n\n`sorry` is permitted in proofs (`theorem` bodies, propositional\n`structure` fields). It is **not** permitted as a data-level body,\nand neither is any other placeholder shape: wrong-but-plausible\ntrivial returns, identity casts, `axiom` stand-ins, native-typed\nfunctions that detour through `Nat`/`Int`, `@[extern]` shims that\ntrampoline back to a Lean fallback, alternative implementations\nwith the wrong complexity. Every committed `def` ships with its\nintended-final implementation. See\n[design-principles.md §7](../SPEC/design-principles.md), the rule\nrestated in [PLAN/Phase1.md](Phase1.md), and the bench-discovery\nrollback path in [SPEC/benchmarking.md](../SPEC/benchmarking.md).\n\nThe word \"scaffold\" in this project refers to *adding a declaration\nto the Lean source*, not to filling that declaration with a stub. A\nscaffolded `def` is fully implemented; only its proofs may be\n`sorry`. Phrases like \"Phase 1 scaffold returns <trivial>\",\n\"scaffold for the eventual <X> bridge\", \"honest placeholder\", and\n\"for now ...\" are forbidden in committed code.\n\n### Fallback discipline for total forms of partial helpers\n\nA close relative of the placeholder rule, applying when a partial\nhelper (`f : T → Option U`) is wrapped into a total form\n(`f' : T → U`) via a fallback in the `none` branch. Allowed only\nwhen the per-library SPEC classifies the fallback under exactly\none of two modes: `unreachable-by-pipeline-invariant` (the bridge\nproves a `_isSome` theorem and the SPEC text cites it by name) or\n`audited-emergency-value` (rare; the SPEC text documents the\nrationale and the call sites that accept the fallback). No third\nmode. *\"Refactor later\"* is not admissible. The full rule lives\nat [design-principles.md §8](../SPEC/design-principles.md). The\nrollback path that applies to a benchmark-discovered scaffolding\n`def` applies here too.\n\n### Headline correctness theorem (per-library, `done_through ≥ 4`)\n\nEvery library that bumps `done_through` past Phase 4 must carry, in\nits Mathlib bridge, a single headline correctness theorem stating\nthe end-to-end post-condition of its public API. The per-library\nSPEC names the theorem (typically in a `## Headline correctness\ntheorem` section) and the semantic clauses it must establish.\nIntermediate lemmas are admissible when they are either\n(a) load-bearing for some proof of the headline theorem, or\n(b) independently justified as public API, executable checker, or\nregression guard with stated rationale; collections of intermediate\nlemmas that don't compose into the headline theorem are dead\nweight and block the `done_through` bump. The orchestrator\ndispatches the headline theorem as the critical-path artefact, not\narbitrary bags of per-function obligations that contribute to it.\n\n### Read the SPEC, not just the issue body\n\nWhen you pick up an issue, re-read every SPEC file linked in its\n**Context** section in full before starting. Issue bodies paraphrase;\nthe SPEC is the source of truth.\n\nRead adjacent SPEC and PLAN files when they look plausibly relevant —\nsibling library SPECs, `SPEC/design-principles.md`, the Phase K\nconventions for your phase. The extra tokens are cheap.\n\n### PR workflow\n\nEvery PR gets auto-merge at creation:\n\n```bash\ngh pr create --title \"…\" --body \"…\"\ngh pr merge \"$(gh pr view --json number --jq .number)\" --auto --squash\n```\n\nAt the start of every planner cycle, merge all mergeable+green open\nPRs before creating new work. Downstream agents are blocked on `main`\nuntil merged PRs land.\n\n`main` is branch-protected: auto-merge only fires once every required\nstatus check (`build`, `build-macos`, `conformance`) is green. CI\ngating is non-negotiable — see [SPEC/CI.md](../SPEC/CI.md).\n\n### CI work expansion\n\nWhen adding a new conformance check, oracle, benchmark, or build\ntarget, **extend the script of the existing single ubuntu job** in\nthe relevant workflow (`conformance.yml` for conformance/oracle work,\n`ci.yml` for build/check/benchmark work). Do **not** add a new\ntop-level job, a new `strategy.matrix`, or a new workflow file. The\nfull rationale and the trigger / concurrency / Mathlib-cache rules\nevery workflow must satisfy live in [SPEC/CI.md](../SPEC/CI.md); read\nit before editing any file under `.github/workflows/`.\n\nFor benchmark targets specifically, the structural and timing rules\nin [SPEC/benchmarking.md §Mathlib-free benches](../SPEC/benchmarking.md)\nand the \"Time budget\" subsection of\n[SPEC/benchmarking.md §CI integration](../SPEC/benchmarking.md)\nconstrain what a new bench is allowed to look like (no Mathlib in\nthe link chain; per-library smoke warn at 30 s; repo-wide hard cap).\n\n---\n\n## Lexical conventions\n\n### Naming\n\n| Context | Convention | Example |\n|---------|-----------|---------|\n| SPEC library files | kebab-case | `hex-poly-z-mathlib.md` |\n| Lean modules/dirs | PascalCase | `HexPolyZMathlib` |\n| `libraries.yml` keys | PascalCase | `HexPolyZMathlib:` |\n| `lakefile.lean` names | PascalCase | `lean_lib HexPolyZMathlib where` |\n\nThe PascalCase form is a direct transliteration of the kebab-case\nname: each hyphen-separated segment becomes capitalised and joined\n(`hex-poly-z-mathlib` → `HexPolyZMathlib`).\n\n**Acronym exception.** Segments that are recognised acronyms keep\nall their letters upper-case rather than capitalising only the first.\nThe current acronym list is:\n\n| kebab segment | PascalCase form |\n|---------------|-----------------|\n| `gf2`         | `GF2`           |\n| `lll`         | `LLL`           |\n| `gfq`         | `GFq`           |\n| `fp`          | `Fp`            |\n| `crt`         | `CRT`           |\n\nSo `hex-gf2` → `HexGF2`, `hex-lll` → `HexLLL`, `hex-lll-mathlib`\n→ `HexLLLMathlib`, `hex-gfq-field` → `HexGFqField`. (`gfq` is\n\"Galois field GF(q)\" where `q = p^n` — the `q` is a variable, so\nlower-case; `fp` is \"F_p\" with `p` variable, lower-case.)\n\nExtend this table when adding libraries whose names involve further\nacronyms. Do not silently introduce a mixed-case spelling.\n\n### Process vocabulary stops at the issue boundary\n\nIssue and PR titles may use scheduling shorthand (\"HO-1 Gap 1\nconsumer\"); the Lean identifiers a worker creates name the\n**mathematics**, never the issue's process words — `ZPoly.toMonic`,\nnot `monicisedCoreTransportPackage`. See\n[design-principles.md §Naming and documentation](../SPEC/design-principles.md).\n\n### FFI\n\nLibraries that use `@[extern]` (e.g. `hex-arith` for GMP wrappers,\n`hex-gf2` for CLMUL) keep their C shims in a `ffi/` subdirectory\nwithin the library (e.g. `HexArith/ffi/wide_arith.c`). Compile those\nsources in `lakefile.lean` via an `extern_lib` block; use\n`moreLinkArgs` only for system linker flags such as `-lgmp`, never for\nlisting `.c` sources.\n\nFor ad hoc interpreter smoke tests of extern-backed declarations, use\n`lake lean <file>` (or pass the module dynlibs explicitly via\n`--load-dynlib`). Plain `lake env lean <file>` imports the workspace's\n`.olean` files but does not auto-load per-module dynlibs, so `#eval`\nof `@[extern]` declarations can fail with `Could not find native\nimplementation`.\n\n### Library umbrella discipline\n\nEach library `Foo` has a root file `Foo.lean` (the *umbrella*) and a\ndirectory `Foo/` containing its modules. The umbrella **must import\nevery regular module** under `Foo/`. The only exempt files are those\ndeclared as `lean_exe` roots in `lakefile.lean` (typically\n`Foo/Bench.lean` and `Foo/EmitFixtures.lean`).\n\nWhy this matters: with `precompileModules := true` (the default), Lake\nbuilds a per-library shared object `libHex_Foo.dylib`/`.so` from the\n`.c.o.export` files of the modules the umbrella imports, and lists\nthat shared object as a plugin when elaborating any downstream library.\nA module file under `Foo/` that the umbrella does **not** import is\nabsent from the shared object even though it is imported individually\nby downstream code. On Linux, flat-namespace lazy binding hides the\ngap by resolving the missing symbols against per-module dylibs that\nhappen to be loaded for the same elaboration run. On macOS, dyld's\nstricter symbol resolution aborts with `dyld[..]: missing symbol\ncalled` on the first downstream elaboration that needs the absent\nsymbols. This is exactly the failure mode that motivated adding macOS\nto CI in [Phase0.md §6](Phase0.md).\n\n`scripts/check_dag.py` enforces this rule mechanically: every\n`Foo/X.lean` is either listed as a `lean_exe` root in `lakefile.lean`\nor imported (directly or transitively) by `Foo.lean`. Any PR that adds\na new module under `Foo/` must also update `Foo.lean` (or chain the\nimport through an existing intermediate module).\n\n---\n\n## Issue creation\n\nThe project stays **GitHub-native** for orchestration. The canonical\ntask tracker is GitHub issues plus whatever structured fields GitHub\nProjects or issue forms can provide. Do not introduce a separate\ncommitted task-graph file that has to be kept in sync with issues.\n\nPrefer the **issue body** over custom GitHub metadata unless there\nis a clear need for the metadata. The issue body should contain the\ncanonical task description in a stable, easy-to-scan format.\n\n### Narrow, not umbrella\n\n- Use **many narrow issues** rather than a few large umbrella issues.\n- Prefer issues scoped to one API surface, one proof cluster, one\n  algorithmic subcomponent, or one benchmark/conformance target.\n- Large umbrella issues are fine for human orientation, but\n  execution should happen in smaller child or blocking issues.\n\nGood issue sizes include:\n\n- one major structure plus its immediate API;\n- one SPEC subsection with a coherent implementation target;\n- one theorem cluster that obviously belongs together;\n- one conformance or benchmark slice for a single subsystem.\n\nAvoid issues that mix:\n\n- multiple libraries with weak coupling;\n- implementation plus broad cleanup across unrelated files;\n- an entire library's worth of declarations unless the library is tiny.\n\n### Canonical issue body shape\n\nKeep issue bodies simple Markdown, but standardize the section\nheadings so agents can read and write them consistently. Default\nshape:\n\n- **Current state** — what is already true, and what assumptions the\n  worker should begin from.\n- **Deliverables** — the concrete outputs expected from this issue.\n- **Library placement** — the file path the deliverables will land in,\n  the SPEC § that governs that path (quote 1–3 lines), and a one-line\n  answer to each of the four placement questions in\n  [Library placement is a hard precondition](#library-placement-is-a-hard-precondition).\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}
